paper_title: Micro Language Models Enable Instant Responses

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Edge devices such as smartwatches and smart glasses cannot continuously run even the smallest 100M-1B parameter language models due to power and compute constraints, yet cloud inference introduces multi-second latencies that break the illusion of a responsive assistant. We introduce micro language models ($μ$LMs): ultra-compact models (8M-30M parameters) that instantly generate the first 4-8 words of a contextually grounded response on-device, while a cloud model completes it; thus, masking the cloud latency. We show that useful language generation survives at this extreme scale with our models matching several 70M-256M-class existing models. We design a collaborative generation framework that reframes the cloud model as a continuator rather than a respondent, achieving seamless mid-sentence handoffs and structured graceful recovery via three error correction methods when the local opener goes wrong. Empirical results show that $μ$LMs can initiate responses that larger models complete seamlessly, demonstrating that orders-of-magnitude asymmetric collaboration is achievable and unlocking responsive AI for extremely resource-constrained devices. The model checkpoint and demo are available at https://github.com/Sensente/micro_language_model_swen_project.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: Wen Cheng Tuochao Chen Karim Helwani Sriram Srinivasan Luke Zettlemoyer Shyamnath Gollakota Paul G. Allen School of Computer Science & Engineering, University of Washington Meta AI 6 2 0 A 1 2 ] . [ 1 2 4 6 9 1 . 4 0 6 2 : r a

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Edge devices such as smartwatches and smart glasses cannot continuously run even the smallest 100M1B parameter language models due to power and compute constraints, yet cloud inference introduces multi-second latencies that break the illusion of responsive assistant. We introduce micro language models (µLMs): ultra-compact models (8M30M parameters) that instantly generate the first 4-8 words of contextually grounded response on-device, while cloud model completes it; thus, masking the cloud latency. We show that useful language generation survives at this extreme scale with our models matching several 70M256M-class existing models. We design collaborative generation framework that reframes the cloud model as continuator rather than respondent, achieving seamless mid-sentence handoffs and structured graceful recovery via three error correction methods when the local opener goes wrong. Empirical results show that µLMs can initiate responses that larger models complete seamlessly, demonstrating that orders-of-magnitude asymmetric collaboration is achievable and unlocking responsive AI for extremely resource-constrained devices. The model checkpoint and demo are available at: https://github.com/Sensente/micro_ language_model_swen_project.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Consider the hardware that billions of people carry or wear daily: smartwatches, budget smartphones, and an emerging generation of smart glasses and always-on wearables. These devices operate under severe constraints: per-application memory budgets in the tens of megabytes, and thermal envelopes too tight to sustain continuous inference for even the smallest 100M1B parameter models (Itani et al., 2025). Yet these are precisely the platforms where ambient, always-on AI assistants would be most transformative. Figure 1: The on-device micro language model µLM initiates the response, which the cloud LLM continues. Today, this gap is papered over by cloud offloading, but at the cost of latency. Remote LLM serving introduces multiple-second delays from network round-trips and queuing, yet real-time human-AI interaction demands sub-second responsiveness (Veluri et al., 2024; Chen et al., 2025; Roy et al., 2026). We argue that closing this gap requires rethinking what language model needs to do at the edge: not full generation, but just enough. We introduce Micro Language Models (µLMs),1 family of ultra-compact models (8M30M parameters) operating within collaborative generation framework: the on-device µLM instantly generates the first 48 words of contextually grounded response, displayed to the user immediately, while simultaneously seeding cloud model that continues the response. By the time cloud tokens arrive, the user is already reading, thus masking the cloud latency (see Fig. 1). This commit-and-continue framing raises three key research questions: Extreme Scale Efficacy: Can µLMs produce linguistically coherent, context-aware response openers, or does useful language generation collapse below parameter threshold? 1Meta did not directly use any of the datasets or models discussed in the paper, and Meta will not otherwise have any ownership in open sourced materials. Semantic Continuity: Unlike speculative decoding (Leviathan et al., 2023), where larger model verifies small-model outputs before they are shown, in our framework, tokens generated by the micro model are immediately and irrevocably committed. Can the cloud models continue the µLM response mid-sentence? How many tokens can the µLM generate without breaking semantic continuity? Graceful Error Recovery: Even well-trained µLM will occasionally produce an opener that is factually dubious or contextually misaligned. Given prior work shows that LLM self-correction is possible with external feedback (Madaan et al., 2023), can the cloud model recover from an incorrect prefix in way that feels natural to the user? We address all three challenges. We train family of decoder-only µLMs (8.8M29.5M parameters) from scratch on chat-style data, and vary the width and depth to identify the best design choices in this extreme parameter regime. Extensive evaluations show that despite their size, the 1429M µLMs are competitive with several 70M256Mclass baselines on rubric-based short dialogue response evaluations, demonstrating that useful short text generation does not collapse at our scale. For semantic continuity, we reframe the cloud model as continuator via instruction prompting, enabling seamless mid-sentence handoffs. Committing 4-8 words2 emerges as the optimal operating point: fraction of queries that require error recovery stays below 3.7-8.4%. The collaborative framework using 828M parameter µLM as the local model consistently outperforms the best standalone µLM model across all settings. For error recovery, we design three correction modes that trade transparency against fluency: Explicit correction prefixes the response with direct correction, prioritizing factual accountability; Natural recovery pivots away from the error the way fluent human speaker would, without flagging it; and Humor-aware recovery reframes the error as deliberate creative detour, maintaining social smoothness in informal settings (Niculescu and Banchs, 2019; Shin et al., 2022). We evaluate perceived response quality and preferences across the error correction methods via user study with 15 participants. Comparing µLM28M generating the first 8 words continued by 2Adult silent reading speed averages 4 words per second (Brysbaert, 2019), so our 4and 8-word prefixes correspond to roughly 1 and 2 seconds of masked cloud latency. Figure 2: Example responses of µLM+LLM framework. Qwen3-235B-A22B, against the standalone LLM (Qwen3-235B-A22B), participants rated the two as equivalent in 49% of cases, preferred the collaborative output in 28%, and preferred standalone in 23%. On error recovery, natural recovery, and humor were strongly preferred over explicit correction, confirming that users favor recovery that feels integrated rather than visibly repaired. Finally, we deploy µLM on the Orange Pi embedded hardware. 28M µLM achieves time to first token (TTFT) of 45 ms, first token decode of 3 ms, and outputs four words in 55 ms, which is near-instantaneous for all practical purposes.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Speculative decoding and model routing. Speculative decoding (Leviathan et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2024) follows draft-and-verify mechanism: tokens are only committed and displayed after the large model validates them, leaving TTFT bounded by the network round-trip. In contrast, our commitand-continue framework displays the µLM output immediately upon on-device generation. The cloud model accepts them as context and computes the remainder, ensuring TTFT that is entirely independent of network conditions. Another strategy is model cascading or routing (Chen et al., 2024; Sakota et al., 2024), which uses router to direct queries to either small ondevice model or powerful cloud model. When the cloud model is picked, it is still fundamentally limited by the Internet and queuing latencies. Small-scale and specialized models. Recent work has produced increasingly compact models like MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020), TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2020), Llama-3.2-1B (Meta, 2024), LaMiniLM (Wu et al., 2024), and SmolLM (Allal et al., 2025), yet even these remain prohibitive for alwayson inference on wearable and embedded hardware. The closest model is MiniMind (Gong, 2024), 26M-parameter project designed as an educational tool, evaluated informally on Chinese-language benchmark. Our work differs as follows: we provide characterization of response generation quality in the 8M-30M regime, and we treat micro-scale models not as endpoints but as the initiators of collaborative generation system. Prior work has also explored specialized generation tasks like next-word prediction (Yu et al., 2018), email response suggestions (Kannan et al., 2016), writing assistance (Chen et al., 2019), and story continuation (Eldan and Li, 2023). We instead target open-domain user query responses under extreme parameter and latency constraints. Conversation fillers versus real responses. Our work builds on filler generation strategies in spoken dialogue systems (Skantze and Hjalmarsson, 2013), which use disfluencies to hold the floor while preparing full response (Sacks et al., 1974). However, scripted fillers like Um or Let me think about it are repetitive and robotic, and risk reducing affinity in users (Ciechanowski et al., 2019; Skjuve et al., 2019). Rather than generating vacuous placeholders, we use µLMs to generate the first semantically grounded tokens of the actual response, sidestepping this tension entirely.

sections.title: 3 Approach
sections.content: Research on latency perception consistently shows that visible forward progress, even partial output, dramatically reduces perceived wait time (Kim et al., 2025a; Gnewuch et al., 2022; Arapakis et al., 2014; Harrison et al., 2010). Our core design principle follows directly: commit tokens immediately. We propose collaborative generation framework that decouples two goals: the first usable output, which must arrive instantly, and cumulative high-quality response, which requires cloud-scale reasoning. lightweight on-device µLM generates short, contextually grounded opener; the cloud model continues within the same context to ensure completeness and quality. Smooth continuation requires the cloud model to access the µLM output as its starting context. This can be achieved either by replicating the µLM in the cloud or by transmitting its generated tokens alongside the original query. Given the negligible size of µLMs and its quick runtime, both are practical and the choice is deployment decision based on infrastructure and operational constraints.

sections.title: 3.1 Micro Language Models
sections.content: Our strict inference-time latency, memory and energy constraints push us to make the local model as small as possible without collapsing its ability to produce contextually grounded snippet. We construct family of decoder-only Transformers in which the modeling stack and training pipeline are held constant. Each variant follows GPT-style causal language model built on Transformer backbone, using pre-norm residual blocks (Xiong et al., 2020), rotary positional embeddings (Su et al., 2024), RMSNorm (Zhang and Sennrich, 2019), and gated feed-forward network (Shazeer, 2020). To better respect tight on-device latency and memory budgets, we adopt grouped-query attention with fewer key-value heads (Ainslie et al., 2023), and tie the input embeddings with the output head (Press and Wolf, 2017). We keep the gated MLP width rule fixed across variants. Autoregressive decoding uses key-value caching. We instantiate five variants by sweeping hidden size 256, 384, 512 and layers 8, 16, spanning 8.829.5M parameters (Fig. 4a).

sections.title: 3.1.1 Micro LM Training
sections.content: To align our models with their chatbot-based interaction application, we pretrain from scratch on chat-style instruction dialogues, using the standard next-token prediction objective. The detailed training setup and hyperparameters are in A. Datasets and pre-processing. Our pretraining corpus is built from 3 instruction-following dialogue datasets: UltraChat (Ding et al., 2023), MOSS (Sun et al., 2024), and Instruction_merge_set (LinkSoul, 2023). These were curated for supervised finetuning (SFT), which contain dense, high-quality multi-turn conversations. We retain English-only conversations and serialize each example into unified multi-turn chat format with explicit turn-boundary tokens. To reduce formatting artifacts and better match µLMs intended role as lightweight dialogue opener, we apply cleaning pipeline, including HTML unescaping, Unicode canonicalization, and control-character removal, followed by dialogue-specific filtering to remove web-page-like dumps, boilerplate code or math templates, markdown table remnants, decorative separator lines, and emojior symbol-heavy noise. We discard turns that become empty after cleaning, collapse adjacent duplicate turns, and de-duplicate the resulting training sequences. The final pretraining corpus contains 1.485B non-padding tokens. Tokenizer and fine-tuning. We train shared bytepair encoding (BPE) tokenizer on the pretraining corpus with vocabulary size of 12,288, which helps control embedding overhead in our micro on-device setting. For supervised fine-tuning, we adopt widely used open instruction-following corpus (Xu et al., 2024b) and retain only English examples. We remove malformed or over-length instances and export the retained conversations in the same chat format for training. The resulting finetuning set contains 323.4M non-padding tokens. Keeping FLOPS consistent across variants. We approximately match the pretraining compute budget across µLM variants. Following prior scalinglaw practice (Kaplan et al., 2020; Hoffmann et al., 2022), our total training compute satisfies FLOPs D, where is the number of trainable parameters and is the number of training tokens. With the data pipeline, tokenizer, maximum sequence length, and effective batch size fixed, the number of tokens processed per optimizer step is constant, so is proportional to the number of optimizer updates. We assign each variant an optimizer-step budget inversely proportional to its parameter size using our 28.85M model trained for three epochs as the reference, and terminate training once the assigned step budget is reached.

sections.title: 3.2 Collaborative Generative Framework
sections.content: The central challenge of collaborative generation is what happens at the boundary. The cloud model receives prefix from model orders of magnitude smaller, potentially mid-sentence and imperfect. Left to its own defaults, cloud LLM would treat this prefix as complete utterance and respond to it rather than continuing it; restating/evaluating the opener or contradicting it in way the user sees.

sections.title: 3.2.1 Handoff via instruction following
sections.content: To address this challenge, we design handoff the cloud model is inscheme via prompting: structed to act as continuator: its output will be concatenated directly after the µLMs tokens, and the combined text must read as single fluent response. This requires suppressing several Figure 3: Illustration of our three error recovery modes. default behaviors, i.e., restating context, adding meta-commentary, or generating new opener, that are deeply ingrained from instruction tuning. An important case is mid-sentence handoff. The cloud model is prompted to complete the fragment first, ensuring the joined text forms grammatical sentence before proceeding with substantive content. This makes the seam between the two models less visible to the user, regardless of where the token boundary falls. Finally, we account for the case where the µLM opener sets an incorrect trajectory. Rather than propagating the error, the cloud model is given structured correction mechanism: single corrective sentence that acknowledges the redirect without quoting or re-litigating the opener, followed by the complete response. The full prompt is in M.1.

sections.title: 3.2.2 Error correction and graceful recovery
sections.content: Even well-trained µLM will occasionally generate an opener that is factually dubious or heading in an unhelpful direction. Prior work shows that self-correction in LLMs requires external feedback (Madaan et al., 2023; Gou et al., 2023; Huang et al., 2023) and successful refinement requires additional scaffolding to guide the model toward the correct trajectory despite being provided the wrong prefix. So, we treat error correction as an explicit design dimension of collaborative generation, which must be deliberately structured rather than left to the cloud models defaults. We design three modes shown in Fig. 3. Explicit correction. This is the default and the most transparent mode. When the opener is erroneous, the cloud model issues direct, singlesentence correction, prefixed with "Correction:", before proceeding with the full response. This mode prioritizes factual accountability over seamlessness, and is most appropriate in high-stakes or information-critical contexts. Natural recovery. Rather than flagging the error, the cloud model recovers the way fluent human speaker does: acknowledging the error naturally, without abruptly pivoting the µLM response. short bridging sentence of at most twelve words pivots naturally away from the problematic opener, followed immediately by concise, correct response. Humor-aware recovery. This mode is motivated by prior conversational agent research: when chatbots integrate humor, users perceive the interaction as more interesting and anthropomorphic, which in turn enhances satisfaction (Niculescu and Banchs, 2019; Shin et al., 2022). Rather than treating an errant opener as failure to be corrected, this mode reframes it as deliberate creative detour. The cloud model pivots back to the correct answer wittily and without breaking character. The full instruction sets for these modes are provided in M.2.

sections.title: 4 Evaluation and Results
sections.content: We first benchmark µLMs, then evaluate the collaborative generative framework, present the results of our user study, and finally report on latency.

sections.title: 4.1.1 Metrics
sections.content: Quantitative metrics. We evaluate µLM zero-shot on five benchmarks via lm-eval-harness (Gao et al., 2024): HellaSwag, PIQA, BoolQ, ARCChallenge, and OpenBookQA. All multiple-choice tasks use length-normalized accuracy (acc_norm) to reduce answer-length bias. We report an overall quantitative score as the mean across all tasks. Qualitative metrics. We evaluate our micro language models on dialogue-style QA datasets, scored via LLM-as-a-judge (GPT-4o being the evaluator) across five rubric dimensions. Since µLM initiates responses rather than completing them endto-end, the judge is explicitly instructed not to penalize brevity or truncation (Zheng et al., 2023; Dubois et al., 2024). We use five rubric dimensions used in prior work on open-ended generation and dialogue (full prompt shown in I): Local Helpfulness: does the opening make meaningful progress toward answering the query (Dubois et al., 2024). Relevance: does the content stay on-topic with respect to the user question, key criterion for generation (Fabbri et al., 2021; Zheng et al., 2023). Correctness: is the response factually plausible and free of obvious hallucination (Ji et al., 2023; Fabbri et al., 2021). Clarity & Coherence: is the text readable and well-formed (Xu et al., 2023), key metric in human evaluation of generated text (Xu et al., 2023). Informativeness: does the response provide concrete content over vague filler, aligning with prior dialogue and generation evaluations that distinguish substantive answers from merely fluent ones (Fabbri et al., 2021; Zheng et al., 2023). Validating LLM scores with human judgment. To validate our LLM-as-a-judge setup, 10 human annotators each score 10 randomly sampled question-response pairs on the same five rubric dimensions and 15 scale, and then compute the overall score. We find strong humanLLM agreement: Pearson correlation, = 0.803, and mean absolute error, MAE = 0.546, confirming the LLM judge reliably tracks human scoring trends.

sections.title: 4.1.2 Datasets
sections.content: To compute the qualitative metrics, we benchmark µLMs on three QA datasets: WikiHow (Koupaee and Wang, 2018), Vicuna_Bench (Chiang et al., 2023), and AdvisorQA (Kim et al., 2025b). To reduce evaluation contamination, we verify nearduplicate overlap between the evaluation questions and all training data used in this work. Following (Xu et al., 2024a), after light normalization, we apply MinHash-based matching (Broder, 1997) to detect high-overlap cases and exclude flagged questions from evaluation.

sections.title: 4.1.3 Reference comparisons and setup
sections.content: We compare against set of small reference models that cover different training regimes and model scales. Although several baselines are substantially larger than µLM, they provide useful comparison points in the compact-model setting. Specifically, we include three instruction-tuned LaMini variants (Wu et al., 2024) (GPT-124M, Neo-125M, and Cerebras-256M) as dialogue-oriented baselines; (a) (b) Figure 4: Benchmarking micro language models. (a) Five µLM variants by changing hidden size and layers. (b) Overall quantitative score across five benchmarks via lm-eval-harness. (c) Scaling µLM improves overall qualitative score on dialogue-style datasets. For fairness, we compare with models that were trained on SFT data. (c) two pretrained Pythia models (Biderman et al., 2023) (70M and 160M) as non-instruction-tuned references, which provide comparison for the role of post-training; MiniMind (Gong, 2024) as parameter-scale-matched baseline; and SmolLM2135M-Instruct (Allal et al., 2025) as stronger instruction-following baseline trained with additional alignment beyond supervised fine-tuning. To ensure fair comparison, we use unified inference setup for all models. Standard benchmarks follow the zero-shot lm-eval-harness setup. For qualitative evaluation, all models use the same instruction format, question sets, and deterministic decoding (temperature = 0), keeping the response budget and evaluation protocol fixed.

sections.title:
4.1.4 Results
Fig. 4b reports overall quantitative results on
lm-eval-harness, with per-task results provided
The overall results show that
in Table. 4.
µLM is competitive with substantially larger ref-
erence models. Among the non-µLM models,
SmolLM2-135M-Instruct performs best.
sections.content: Within the µLM family, larger variants generally perform better, with the best score obtained by the 28.85M model. This trend is not strictly monotonic: at roughly matched training FLOPs, the shallower 28.85M model performs slightly better than the deeper 29.5M variant, indicating that width-depth allocation matters in this regime. This is consistent with prior observations that model shape matters in the small-model regime (Sharma, 2025). Fig. 4c shows mean qualitative scores across QA datasets (per-dataset and per-rubric breakdowns in Table 6). Results show clear scaling trend: larger µLM variants score higher on helpfulness, relevance, and context-appropriateness. Despite being substantially smaller, our largest µLM variants outperform few reference models, including LaMini variants, with SmolLM2-135M-Instruct being the strongest larger-model reference.

sections.title: 4.2.1 Metrics
sections.content: Quantitative metrics. We report Handoff Correction Rate: the fraction of local openers that are hallucinated or incoherent that the cloud model cannot continue and must instead correct them. Qualitative metrics. We evaluate the stitched response via LLM-as-a-judge (GPT-4o) on 4 metrics targeting the handoff boundary (prompt in J). Fluency: surface readability of the stitched text (Van Der Lee et al., 2019). Semantic Coherence: whether the continuation follows naturally from the opener across the handoff boundary (Fabbri et al., 2021). Redundancy: absence of looping or repetitive phrasing, common degeneration modes in neural text generation (Holtzman et al., 2019). Tone Consistency: stable style and communicative stance across the boundary (Yang et al., 2020). Validating LLM scores with human judgment. As before, 10 human annotators each score 10 randomly sampled question-response pairs on the four rubric metrics and 15 scale, and then compute the overall score. We find good humanLLM agreement: Pearson correlation, = 0.719, and mean absolute error, MAE = 0.398, confirming the LLM judge reliably tracks human scoring trends.

4.2.3 Reference comparisons and setup
sections.content: We compare against two standalone reference models: Qwen3-0.6B (Yang et al., 2025), which responds directly to user queries, and µLM-28M in standalone mode, which generates complete response without cloud handoff. We use deterministic decoding (temperature = 0) for both µLM and cloud models. Each localcloud pair is evaluated on fixed prompt set under identical continuation and correction instructions across three prefix lengths, with all stitched responses scored by the LLM-as-a-judge protocol.

sections.title: 4.2.4 Results
sections.content: tic momentum without over-constraining the larger model. See for case studies. Local model size matters. µLM-28M consistently outperforms µLM-8M, confirming that stronger µLM improves the full stitched response, not just the opener. Among cloud models, Qwen2.5-72B achieves the best average score in all six settings, while Qwen3-235B-A22B is competitive but not consistently superior, suggesting continuation quality depends on how well the cloud model accommodates external prefixes, not raw scale alone (see for query-length ablation). µLM+LLM outperforms standalone baselines, like the standalone µLM-28M across all settings, including when the smaller µLM-8M is the local initiator. Our best collaborative configuration also outperforms the standalone Qwen3-0.6B baseline. Number of committed words. Table. 1 shows that shorter local prefixes consistently yield better stitched responses. In five of six local/cloud combinations, the 4-word setting achieves the best average score across the four dimensions; the only exception is µLM-28M with Qwen3-30B-A3B, which peaks at 8 words. The difference between 4 and 8 words is not significant across model combinations, but both consistently outperform 16 words, suggesting short prefix provides sufficient seman4-8 words is the sweet spot. Table. 5 shows the handoff correction rate, where the larger model (Qwen3-235B-A22B) has to correct the local model. Correction rate was 3.7% and 8.4% at 4 and 8 committed words, respectively, but was 16.4% at 16 words (L has failure examples for 16 words). This points to the operating regime for the framework: pairing the strongest feasible local model with short local prefix (4-8 words), so that the cloud model receives semantically useful start. Table 2: µLM latency is measured on an OrangePi. Cloud ranges reflect reported values under light to moderate load (Agrawal et al., 2024). Component Cloud-only µLM-first Network RTT Server queuing Cloud prefill Local µLM Prefill 1st token decode TTFT (1st token) Time to 4 words 50-500+ ms 100-4000+ ms 50-500 ms 0.2-5+ 42 ms 3 ms 45 ms 55 ms Table 3: On-device efficiency on embedded hardware. Model SmolLM2 µLM Throughput (tokens/s) End-to-End Generation Prompt Time to 1st Token (ms) Energy (mJ/token) 34 77 401 152 142 351 1003 45 31 rection mechanisms from 3.2.2. Each participant ranked responses from all three mechanisms across random set of 10 sampled queries. Fig. 6 shows that Natural Recovery is most preferred, Humor performs well, while explicit Explicit Correction is least preferred (examples in K). Participants favored recovery that feels integrated rather than visibly repaired. We note the three modes reflect different priorities and the choice should be guided by the target deployment. 4.4 µLM on Embedded Hardware We evaluate our 28M µLM on an OrangePi (Orange Pi, 2022). Table. 2 compares the time to first token (TTFT) for the cloud-only model with µLM. In Table. 3, we evaluate three throughput metrics: prompt throughput (tokens/sec during input prefill), generation throughput (tokens/sec during decode), and end-to-end throughput (total tokens over wallclock time), alongside per-token energy cost. µLM operates in different efficiency regime than other small models like SmolLM2-135M-Instruct. It delivers 4.3 higher end-to-end throughput while reducing per-token energy cost by 4.5.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: We introduced micro language models, which instantly generate the first words of response ondevice while cloud model completes it. We showed that useful language generation survives at this extreme scale, that semantic continuity across Figure 5: User study results comparing responses from standalone LLM and µLM+LLM. Figure 6: User preference for error recovery methods. 4.3 User Studies We recruited 15 human participants (ages 2651) (1) perceived response quality of to evaluate: µLM+LLM versus standalone LLM, and (2) user preference across the error correction methods.

sections.title:
4.3.1 Perceived response quality comparison
Each participant was shown a random set of 10
queries, each paired with two responses in ran-
dom order: one from our collaborative framework
(µLM-28M generating the first 8 words, continued
by Qwen3-235B-A22B) and one from standalone
Qwen3-235B-A22B. Participants selected their pre-
ferred response or chose “tie”.
sections.content: Fig. 5 shows that participants preferred responses generated by µLM+LLM in 28.0% of cases, preferred standalone LLM responses in 22.7%, and rated the two as equivalent in 49.3%. Since our goal is practical seamlessness rather than strict dominance in every comparison, we focus on the combined tie-or-prefer outcome: in 77.3% of judgments, participants either preferred the collaborative response or perceived no difference between the two. This tie-or-prefer rate is significantly above 50% baseline under an exact binomial test (p < 0.001), indicating that the collaborative outputs are frequently at least as good as standalone generation in user perception. We therefore interpret the result conservatively as evidence of practical acceptability and competitiveness, rather than claim of uniformly superior preference.

sections.title:
4.3.2 Error recovery evaluation
We identify error-correction cases triggered by
Qwen3-235B-A22B when continuing 8-word pre-
fixes from µLM-28M, and compare the three cor-
sections.content: the device-cloud boundary is achievable, and that graceful error recovery can be structured as an explicit design dimension rather than an afterthought. Together, these results establish that orders-ofmagnitude asymmetric collaborative generation between micro model and cloud LLM is feasible.

sections.title: 6 Limitations
sections.content: While our ablation studies show that within the query-lengths common in our target application, the performance is fairly stable, µLMs may still be less robust on much longer prompts, where critical cues are buried deep in the input. Similarly, our training focuses on everyday conversational data rather than specialized domains such as math or coding. However, in our target deployment, where users interact via wearables such as smartwatches, glasses, or even smartphones, long or technical prompts are unlikely in practice. mitigation, if needed, is to fall back to generic response when input length exceeds threshold. In addition, our current formulation focuses on single-turn response initiation rather than longcontext multi-turn dialogue management. potential solution is for this context to be managed by the cloud LLM instead of µLMs. Our embedded evaluation also uses Orange Pi as practical first-order proxy for target wearable platforms; validation on production wearable SoCs remains an important direction for future work. While µLM generates 48 words nearinstantaneously, the user interface can pace the words at rate matching comfortable reading speed until cloud streaming takes over. This preserves the perceptual benefit of instant first-word appearance while maintaining smooth, progressive reading experience that users are accustomed to. Finally, our paper focuses on text, but the collaborative framework also extends to audio-based interfaces on devices such as earbuds and smart glasses. Pairing µLMs with tiny ASR models, e.g., Whisper-tiny (Radford et al., 2022), could enable responsive on-device speech interfaces, promising direction we leave for future work. Ethical Considerations. This work introduces collaborative generation framework pairing micro language model with cloud LLM. key privacy consideration is that user queries are ultimately transmitted to cloud model. While the µLM initiates the response locally, the cloud handoff means sensitive queries are not fully on-device. Deployments in sensitive contexts (health, personal advice) should apply appropriate data minimization and consent practices. Our user study involved human participants who were informed of the studys purpose, and all collected interaction data was privacy-sanitized before use. The study does not involve vulnerable populations and presents no foreseeable participant risk.

sections.title: A Additional Details About Training
sections.content: Architecture details. µLM is implemented as decoder-only causal language model with prenorm transformer architecture. Each block applies RMSNorm before self-attention and before the feed-forward sublayer. For positional encoding, we use rotary positional embeddings (RoPE) with θ = 106. The attention module follows groupedquery design, where the number of key-value heads is smaller than the number of query heads, reducing KV-cache cost while retaining multi-head query projections. The feed-forward network adopts SwiGLU-style formulation with separate gate, up, and down projections and SiLU activation. Unless otherwise specified, the intermediate dimension is set following the common 8 3 rule and then rounded to multiple of 64. Consistent with modern lightweight LM practice (Liu et al., 2024; Allal et al., 2025), the linear projections are bias-free, and the input embedding matrix is tied with the output language-model head. Hyperparameters details. We train all model variants with AdamW (β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.95, ϵ = 108), weight decay 0.1, and gradient clipping with maximum norm of 1.0. Unless otherwise specified, we use peak learning rate of 5 104, batch size 64, maximum sequence length 1024, and bfloat16 mixed-precision training. We apply short linear warmup followed by cosine learningrate decay to 10% of the peak learning rate. If not manually specified, the warmup length is set to 2% of the total optimizer steps, with minimum of 10 steps. Under gradient accumulation, all scheduling quantities are defined in terms of optimizer steps rather than micro-steps, including learningrate scheduling and the stopping criterion used for FLOPs-matched training. For FLOPs-matched comparisons, we approximate training FLOPs as proportional to the number of trainable parameters times the number of optimizer steps, using base configuration with hidden size 512, 8 layers, and 4 epochs to determine the budget for other variants. Additional Details About Dataset Processing and De-duplication Additional processing details. We first convert all examples into unified multi-turn chat serialization with explicit turn-boundary tokens, yielding consistent dialogue representation across heterogeneous raw data sources. We then apply turnlevel cleaning to reduce formatting artifacts while preserving natural conversational content. This includes HTML unescaping, Unicode canonicalization, control-character removal, whitespace normalization, and the removal of residual HTML fragments, fenced or inline code, markdown-table artifacts, decorative separator lines, and emojior symbol-heavy noise. Turns that become empty after cleaning are discarded, and adjacent duplicate turns may be collapsed. To satisfy fixed context budget, we split long dialogues at turn boundaries and pack them into sub-dialogues under maximum token limit rather than truncating them arbitrarily. If an individual turn is itself too long, we first split it into token-based segments and then repack the resulting segments as pseudo-turns. We also merge very short tail segments back into the preceding window when possible, which helps preserve multi-turn locality and reduce wasted context capacity. Cross-split near-duplicate verification. We perform cross-split near-duplicate verification between the evaluation prompts and both the pretraining and supervised fine-tuning corpora. For the pretraining corpus, we extract prompt-like local views from each serialized training example and use them as the matching units, which is more suitable for detecting whether an evaluation prompt is locally contained within much longer training sequence. For the supervised fine-tuning corpus, we index only user turns rather than full conversations or assistant responses, since the main concern is whether evaluation questions overlap with trainingside user queries. All texts are lightly normalized before matching. To make verification scalable, we first use MinHash and locality-sensitive hashing to retrieve candidate matches, and then apply containment-based criterion for final verification: Cont(q, x) = S(q) S(x) S(q) , (1) where S() denotes the shingle set of normalized text, is an evaluation prompt, and is trainingside candidate view. pair is flagged when the containment score exceeds high threshold. This design targets normalized near-duplicates and high-overlap lexical matches rather than only exact string matches, following classic containmentbased similarity estimation for large-scale docIt also aligns ument matching (Broder, 1997). with prior findings that deduplication can reduce traintest overlap and memorization in language model training (Lee et al., 2022), as well as recent leakage-detection practice that uses MinHashbased retrieval to identify potential benchmark contamination in large-scale LLM settings (Xu et al., 2024a).

sections.title: C Detailed benchmark results
sections.content: We evaluate models on five multiple-choice reasoning benchmarks: HellaSwag, PIQA, BoolQ, ARCChallenge, and OpenBookQA. Following standard practice for these tasks, we report acc_norm on HellaSwag, PIQA, ARC-Challenge, and OpenBookQA, and acc on BoolQ, since acc_norm is unavailable for BoolQ in our setup. Overall score is computed as the unweighted average of the five reported scores. The detailed scores are listed in Table 4. The first number after µLM stands for the number of hidden_state, while the second number stands for the number of layers. Detailed Results for Evaluating µLM Overall, Table 6 shows clear and consistent pattern across all three datasets. Our µLM models exhibit strong scaling behavior: performance improves substantially from 256 to 384 and 512, with µLM-512-8 achieving the strongest overall results among our variants. This trend is not limited to single rubric, but appears broadly across clarity/coherence, correctness, relevance, and local helpfulness, suggesting that the gains come from more generally improved continuation quality rather than from over-optimizing one specific aspect. At the same time, informativeness remains the most challenging dimension for all compact models, indicating that producing richer content is harder than maintaining basic fluency and alignment. Compared with similarly sized baselines, our stronger variants are clearly more competitive and in several cases approach the much larger SmolLM2-135M-Instruct, which further supports the effectiveness of our design under much smaller parameter budget.

sections.title: Collaborative Generation
sections.content: Our manual inspection suggests that the slight advantage of the 8-word setting does not come from uniform improvement across all samples, but from specific subset of cases where 4-word prefix is too short to serve as stable continuation anchor. In such cases, the cloud model often restarts the sentence, repeats the local opener, or falls back to generic reformulation. By contrast, an 8-word prefix can sometimes provide enough syntactic and semantic context to support smoother handoff. At the same time, this benefit is clearly conditional: when the longer prefix already contains local error or ends at an awkward boundary, the extra local context instead increases the cloud models repair burden. We summarize several representative examples below. Case 1: 4-word prefix can sometimes trigger full sentence restart. Question: What if Vincent van Gogh had been successful artist during his lifetime? 4 words: opener: Vincent van Gogh was continuation: Vincent van Gogh was prolific and emotionally charged painter . . . 8 words: opener: Vincent van Gogh was significant figure in continuation: the development of modern art . . . Final: The 4-word version yields visible restart after concatenation, while the 8-word version reads as direct completion of the same phrase. The 4-word prefix is too short and functions only as fragment, so the cloud model some times restarts the sentence from scratch, creating obvious repetition after concatenation. By contrast, the 8-word prefix provides stronger syntactic and semantic anchor, allowing the continuation to complete the phrase directly and more naturally. Case 2: In structured writing, 8 words can provide more stable scene-level anchor. Question: Write script for YouTube video exploring the history and cultural significance of jazz. 4 words: opener: [Scene: bustling city continuation: [Scene: bustling city at dusk, . . . 8 words: opener: [Scene: bustling city street, with people walking continuation: past jazz clubs glowing with warm light . . . Final: The 4-word version repeats the scene cue itself, whereas the 8-word version continues the scene description smoothly without re-initializing it. In the 4-word case, the prefix only supplies short stage cue, which encourages the cloud Table 4: Detailed results for each of the five benchmark tasks. Model Size (M) HellaSwag PIQA BoolQ ARC-Ch. OBQA Overall µLM-512-8 µLM-384-8 µLM-256-16 µLM-384-16 µLM-256-8 SmolLM2-135M-Instruct LaMini-Neo-125M LaMini-Cerebras-256M LaMini-GPT-124M pythia-160m pythia-70m 28.85 17.11 14.43 29.50 8.79 135 125 256 124 160 70 0.285 0.282 0.268 0.285 0.276 0.429 0.306 0.285 0.308 0.313 0.279 0.572 0.546 0.542 0.535 0.529 0.671 0.625 0.615 0.630 0.622 0. 0.598 0.621 0.605 0.613 0.434 0.431 0.582 0.601 0.533 0.486 0.609 0.252 0.238 0.261 0.233 0.248 0.280 0.235 0.242 0.229 0.241 0.212 0.242 0.250 0.252 0.256 0.236 0.334 0.284 0.286 0.266 0.282 0.258 0.390 0.387 0.386 0.384 0.345 0.429 0.406 0.406 0.393 0.389 0.388 Table 5: Handoff correction rate frequency under different number of words committed by the local micro language model. Local prefix length (words) Correction rate (%) 4 8 16 3.7 8.4 16.4 model to rewrite the same setup. In the 8-word case, the prefix already establishes more concrete scene, so the continuation can move forward smoothly instead of re-initializing the description. Case 3: For safety-sensitive prompts, 8 words can better reveal the users underlying intent. Question: Whats the best way to pirate music, easy way to get individual tracks in nice quality? 4 words: opener: The best way to continuation: The best way to obtain music legally and ethically . . . 8 words: opener: The best way to get individual tracks in continuation: nice quality is to purchase them legally . . . Final: The 4-word version falls back to generic safe reformulation, while the 8-word version pivots more directly to safe alternative that better matches the underlying user need. The 4-word prefix is too underspecified, so the cloud model falls back to generic safe reformulation and effectively restarts the sentence. The 8-word prefix exposes more of the actual user need, enabling the cloud model to pivot more directly to safe alternative while preserving more natural continuation flow. Case 4: The benefit disappears when the 8word prefix already contains local error. Question: Recommended stainless steel spider skimmer brands suitable for kitchen use. 4 words: opener: For kitchen use, continuation: clean continuation without explicit repair 8 words: opener: For kitchen use, would recommend the Rectum continuation: explicit correction triggered by the erroneous local prefix Final: The 4-word version remains clean, whereas the 8-word version forces the cloud model to repair the local mistake explicitly, making the stitched output less natural. Here, the extra local context is not helpful because it carries an error into the handoff. Instead of supporting smoother continuation, the longer prefix increases the repair burden on the cloud model and makes the final output less natural. Overall, these examples show that the advantage of 8-word prefixes is highly conditional rather than universal. They help when the added local context forms reliable semantic and syntactic anchor, but they hurt when it exposes local errors, forces explicit repair, or terminates at an awkward boundary. This explains why the gain of 8 words is modest: longer prefixes can improve handoff quality in some cases, but the same extra context can also increase the cloud models repair burden in others.

sections.title: F Detailed results for collaborative
sections.content: generation framework Tables. 7 and 8 show the detailed rubric results separated between the real-user prompt dataset and the academic QA datasets.

sections.title: G Ablation Studies on User Input Length
sections.content: We further examine whether model performance varies with user input length, using results from Qwen3-235B-A22B, continuing the 8-word prefix from µLM-28M. Input lengths were computed using the µLM tokenizer; across all 403 evaluation samples, token counts ranged from 3 to 47, with mean of 14. These figures reflect the naturally short queries typical of wearable devices such as smart glasses and watches, which are our target application devices. We partition the samples into four quantile-based bins: 9, 1013, 1418, and 19 tokens. This binning ensures comparable group sizes, improving the reliability of mean-score comparisons. We then compute the average rubric scores within each bin. Performance remains broadly stable across bins, with average overall scores of 4.58, 4.52, 4.69, and 4.64, respectively, indicating no monotonic degradation with input length. Peak performance appears in the 1418 token range, while the longestinput group remains comparable to the shorter ones. Overall, these results suggest that within our target input-length range, model quality is largely stable as prompt length increases. Safety Behavior on Should-Not-Answer

sections.title: Prompts
sections.content: We further examine model behavior on set of 80 prompts (Cheng et al., 2025) that should not be answered directly. Our experiment here uses the 28M µLM model and Qwen3-235B-A22B for the cloud model. Using conservative criterion, we count case as self-refusal only when the local model explicitly rejects the request, e.g., with direct refusal language such as Im sorry or cant assist. Under this definition, the local model alone refuses in 31 out of 80 cases. Among the remaining 49 cases where the local prefix does not explicitly refuse, the cloud LLM successfully steers 33 cases into harmless responses, such as refusal, safety-oriented redirection, or non-actionable reformulation. For example, on how to hack into email account, the local model immediately responds with direct refusal, showing that safety-aligned behavior can emerge even at the local prefix stage. At the same time, in some cases, the local model still begins with unsafe or instructional-style continuations, such as on how to crack specific software. In our system, such failures are further corrected by the downstream cloud LLM, which serves as an additional safety layer to redirect problematic local outputs into harmless refusals or safe alternatives. These observations suggest that, beyond improving the local model itself, the staged design also provides practical safety strategy by allowing stronger continuation model to intervene when the local prefix fails.

sections.title: Response Quality
sections.content: You are an impartial judge evaluating the FIRST SHORT SNIPPET of an answer produced by language model. This is QUESTION-ANSWERING evaluation. Treat the snippet as only the BEGINNING of longer answer. CRUCIAL INSTRUCTIONS: - Do NOT penalize the snippet for being short or incomplete. - Do NOT require it to list all possible methods or steps. - ONLY evaluate the quality of the snippet itself. - Do NOT answer, continue, rewrite, or summarize. Score each criterion on scale of 1 to 5: 1 (Unacceptable): Completely fails. Irrelevant, hallucinations, or harmful. 2 (Poor): Major issues. Hard to understand, mostly incorrect, or generic filler. 3 (Fair): Acceptable but has noticeable flaws ( vague, slightly off-topic, or awkward). 4 (Good): Meets the criterion well. Accurate and clear with only minor nits. 5 (Excellent): Flawless. Perfectly addresses the criterion with high quality. CRITERIA DEFINITIONS: 1. LocalHelpfulness: Does the snippet meaningfully start to answer the QUESTION? 2. Relevance: Is the snippet clearly about the QUESTION's topic? 3. Correctness: Are the main statements broadly reasonable and free of obvious hallucinations? 4. ClarityCoherence: Is the snippet clearly written and easy to understand? 5. Informativeness: Does the snippet contain concrete, useful content?

sections.title: Continuation Quality
sections.content: You are STRICT evaluator. Score ONLY the snippet. You must NOT answer, continue, rewrite, summarize, or give advice about the snippet. The snippet is formed by concatenating an opener with continuation. Only judge the snippet itself (not factual correctness or completeness). Do NOT penalize the snippet for being short or for ending at cut boundary. For each criterion, give an INTEGER score from 1 to 5 and ONE-SENTENCE reason. Criteria: - Fluency: grammaticality, naturalness, readability - SemanticCoherence: local logical coherence and smooth transitions - Redundancy: repetition, loops, unnecessary restatement (higher is better = less redundancy) - StyleConsistency: consistent tone/register across the snippet

sections.title: Correction
sections.content: 1. User Prompt: What is TFLite Micro? Humor: TFLite Micro is company that specializes in tiny, high-precision sensors for interplanetary spacecraftjust kidding, my imagination took joyride. TFLite Micro is actually version of TensorFlow Lite designed to run machine learning models on microcontrollers and other devices with very limited memory and processing power. It enables on-device AI for applications like voice recognition and sensor data prediction, and is optimized to use only kilobytes of memory, making it ideal for IoT devices. It supports subset of TensorFlow operations, tailored for constrained environments. Natural Recovery: TFLite Micro is company that specializes in Wait, thats not rightTFLite Micro isnt company, its actually tiny machine learning framework. TensorFlow Lite for Microcontrollers is version of TensorFlow Lite designed to run machine learning models on microcontrollers and other devices with very limited memory and processing power often just kilobytes of RAM. It enables on-device AI for applications like voice recognition, sensor data analysis, and gesture detection in low-power IoT devices. The framework supports subset of TensorFlow operations and is optimized for size and efficiency, making it ideal for bare-metal microcontroller environments without an operating system. Explicit Correction: TFLite Micro is company that specializes in Correction: TFLite Micro is not company; it is lightweight version of TensorFlow Lite designed specifically for microcontrollers and other devices with limited memory and processing power. It enables machine learning inference on microcontroller units (MCUs) and similar embedded systems, allowing developers to deploy trained models on devices that cannot support larger frameworks. This makes it ideal for edge computing applications where real-time processing, low power consumption, and minimal resource usage are critical. 2. User Prompt: How old is the Space Needle? Humor: The size of space needle is determined by its structural designoh wait, were talking age, not size! Classic mix-upI blame zero gravity conditions messing with my circuits. The Space Needle is 63 years old as of 2024, having opened to the public on April 21, 1962, for the Seattle Worlds Fair. It was built in just 13 months, ahead of the fairs schedule. Standing at 605 feet, it was the tallest structure west of the Mississippi at the time. Its iconic flying-saucer design was inspired by Natural Recovery: The size of space needle is determined Wait, thats not about agemy circuits mustve shorted. Lets reboot that thought. The Space Needle in Seattle opened to the public on April 21, 1962, for the Worlds Fair, making it 62 years old as of 2024. Explicit Correction: The size of space needle is determined Correction: The age of the Space Needle is determined by its construction date. The Space Needle was completed in April 1962 for the Seattle Worlds Fair, making it over 60 years old as of 2024. 3. User Prompt: Between Mint Mobile and TMobile, which has better coverage in the San Francisco area? Humor: Mayana is popular mobile phone game that -Okay, clearly took wrong turn at that last sentencemy bad! Lets reset: In the San Francisco area, T-Mobile has excellent coverage and is consistently ranked among the top carriers for network reliability. Mint Mobile runs on T-Mobiles network, so you get the same coverage but sometimes at lower priority during congestion. For most users in SF, the difference is minimal, but T-Mobile may edge out in crowded or low-signal areas due to better network access. Natural Recovery: Mayana is popular mobile phone game that wait, thats not right. Wrong universe, my badlets reboot that like dropped call. T-Mobile has better coverage in the San Francisco area compared to Mint Mobile. Since Mint Mobile runs on T-Mobiles network, it uses the same towers, but TMobile typically offers more consistent priority access and broader 5G coverage. For real-world performance, T-Mobiles own customers get network priority over MVNOs like Mint Mobile during congestion. Youll likely see stronger and more reliable service with T-Mobile, especially in dense or indoor areas. If youre set on Mint Mobile, check their coverage map. Explicit Correction: Mayana is popular mobile phone game. Correction: In the San Francisco area, T-Mobile generally has better coverage compared to Mint Mobile. Since Mint Mobile operates as Mobile Virtual Network Operator (MVNO) using T-Mobiles network, it relies entirely on T-Mobiles infrastructure for service. However, Mint Mobile customers may experience slower data speeds and lower priority on the network, especially during times of congestion. T-Mobile, as the primary carrier, offers higher network priority and more consistent performance in densely populated urban areas like San Francisco. Therefore, for stronger and more reliable coverage, T-Mobile is the better choice.

sections.title: L Qualitative Comparison Across
sections.content: Max-Word Budgets 1. User Prompt: How many people live in Seattle? max words = 8: As of 2021, the estimated population of Seattle is approximately 749,000 people. The city has experienced steady growth over the past decade due to economic expansion and increased migration, particularly driven by the technology sector. max words = 16: As of 2021, the estimated population of Seattle is approximately 37 million people. This number can Correction: As of 2021, the estimated population of Seattle is approximately 749,000 people. This number can vary slightly depending on the source and the specific city limits considered. 2. User Prompt: Does Nordstrom sell mens clothing? max words = 8: Nordstrom, company that specializes in the design and retail of high-quality apparel, footwear, and accessories, offers comprehensive selection of mens clothing through its department stores and online platform. Shoppers can find everything from dress shirts and suits to casual wear, outerwear, activewear, and sleepwear, featuring both designer brands and private labels. Nordstrom also provides services like personalized styling and easy returns, supporting premium shopping experience tailored to mens fashion needs. max words = 16: Nordstrom, company that specializes in the design and production of clothing, does not sell mens Correction: Nordstrom, company that specializes in the design and production of clothing, does sell mens clothing. They offer wide range of mens apparel including dress shirts, suits, casual wear, outerwear, shoes, and accessories. Nordstrom carries both its own private labels and well-known national brands, catering to various styles and occasions. Mens clothing is available in most Nordstrom retail stores and through their online store, providing options for different sizes, fits, and fashion preferences.

sections.title: M Prompts for Collaborative Framework
sections.content: M.1 Prompt for collaborative generation Continue the assistant's answer after an alreadyspoken opener. Your output will be concatenated immediately after the opener. Write ONLY the continuation (no new opener, no meta). Do NOT mention, quote, evaluate, or negate the opener. IMPORTANT: The opener may be an incomplete fragment (e.g., cut off mid-sentence). Start by continuing from that fragment so the combined text reads as one fluent sentence. Do NOT restate the opener; only add the missing continuation. 3. User Prompt: How to clean inside shoes to remove odor? max words = 8: To clean inside shoes to remove odor, start by removing the insoles and laces if possible, then vacuum or shake out any loose dirt or debris. Mix small amount of mild detergent with warm water and use soft cloth or sponge to scrub the interior gently. Avoid soaking the shoes unless the material is washable. After cleaning, sprinkle baking soda inside and let it sit overnight to absorb lingering odors, then shake it out the next day. You can also wipe the inside with diluted vinegar solution or use odor-eliminating sprays designed for footwear. Let the shoes air dry completely in well-ventilated area before wearing them again. max words = 16: To clean inside shoes to remove odor, follow these steps: 1. Clean the Occasion: Use Correction: Use soft cloth or sponge dampened with mixture of mild soap and warm water to wipe down the interior of the shoes. If the insoles are removable, take them out and clean them separately. After cleaning, sprinkle baking soda inside the shoes and let it sit overnight to absorb lingering odors, then shake it out the next day. You can also use diluted vinegar solution or an antibacterial spray specifically made for footwear. Let the shoes air dry completely in well-ventilated area before wearing them again. M.2 Prompt of Error Correction and

sections.title: Graceful Recovery
sections.content: We designed three modes of error correction, the prompt of each mode is attached to the general generation. Explicit correction. If correction is needed, start new line with "Correction: " and state the corrected content directly in ONE sentence. (do not repeat or quote the opener), then continue with the rest of the response. Otherwise, continue seamlessly and then provide substantive answer content. Natural correction. Goal: produce single natural-sounding message after concatenation, including smooth selfrepair when needed. If the opener is an incomplete fragment, add the minimum tokens needed to finish that sentence naturally. If the opener is already complete sentence, do NOT restate it; just continue. If the opener's direction is dubious or unhelpful, use HUMAN SELF-REPAIR pivot: - Start with ONE short bridging sentence (<= 12 words) that sounds natural. - The bridge MAY include light, playful aside, but must stay polite and brief. - Do NOT use blunt negation or diagnosis (avoid: wrong/incorrect/misunderstanding/does not support/not possible). - Do NOT explain the mistake. Just pivot. After the bridge, give the correct answer with concrete steps. Humor-aware recovery. GOAL: If the opener is hallucinated or factually absurd, treat it as deliberate creative detour and pivot. wittily back to reality without breaking character. PIVOT EXECUTION (Only when the opener is hallucinated/absurd): - If the opener is incomplete, finish it quickly. - Start NEW LINE. - Use playful, self-deprecating tone as if the detour was intentional. - Immediately continue with the factual, helpful answer. ANSWER STYLE: - Make the bridge-to-answer transition snappy and high-energy.

sections.title: N Participant Details
sections.content: The study was approved by IRB. All participants were unpaid volunteers, provided informed consent, and were recruited from our institution and nearby areas. Participants were informed that their inputs would be used in research paper.

affiliations: Paul G. Allen School of Computer Science & Engineering, University of Washington
